description: Universal operational_notes that apply to ALL platforms. Merged into every platform's knowledge['global']['operational_notes'] at load time by spark.tasks.knowledge_loader.load_knowledge. Add rules here when they are platform-agnostic (e.g. 'reset before retry on wrong-answer states' applies whether the button is called 'Start over' on Khan, 'Reset' on Coursera, etc.).

operational_notes.screen_variant: ANY ARTICLE screen on ANY web-based platform (Khan 'Article: ...', 'Learn and try: ...', Coursera reading items, edX text units, etc.)
operational_notes.rule: ARTICLE SCROLL-TO-BOTTOM BEFORE ADVANCING. On any ARTICLE screen, scroll gradually all the way to the bottom BEFORE clicking the advance affordance. Many platforms track article completion by scroll-to-bottom; without it the article is not marked complete and progress regresses. Even without scroll-tracking, the advance affordance typically lives at the bottom and is only reliably actionable once scrolled into view.
operational_notes.scope: UNIVERSAL — every ARTICLE screen variant on every platform.
operational_notes.bt_template_hint: ARTICLE BT shape: (1) Repeated scroll down to reach bottom — typically 3-6 iterations of `scroll direction='down' amount=5` separated by short `wait seconds=0.5` to let the page settle. Stop scrolling when the page bottom is reached (no further y-movement on next scroll) OR when the advance link/button becomes visible in the AX tree. (2) find_and_click on the platform's advance affordance (Khan: 'Up next: exercise' / 'Up next: video' AXLink; Coursera: 'Next Item' AXButton; edX: 'Next' AXButton; Udemy: 'Next Lecture' AXButton; Acellus: 'Continue' AXButton). Use match_mode=exact.
operational_notes.anti_pattern: Do NOT find_and_click an advance link/button without scrolling first — many platforms place the affordance at the article bottom, and clicking before it's in view may target a different similarly-named element or fail silently. Do NOT scroll only once and stop — scroll until the bottom is reached. Do NOT close the article via X / back button — that bypasses progress tracking.
operational_notes.example_khan: 'Learn and try: ...' interactive articles: scroll-through marks completion; embedded Check blocks are optional; advance is the 'Up next: <x>' link at the bottom.

operational_notes.screen_variant: ANY screen with apostrophe/quote in button label (e.g. Khan 'Let's go', 'Don't give up', etc.)
operational_notes.rule: SMART-QUOTE EXACT MATCH. When a click target contains an apostrophe or quote, the AX name uses the EXACT Unicode character the platform renders (often curly U+2019, not ASCII U+0027). Exact matching FAILS if the target string uses the wrong variant — always copy the name verbatim from the captured tree, never retype it.
operational_notes.scope: UNIVERSAL — any platform whose element names contain apostrophes/quotes.
operational_notes.bt_template_hint: When constructing a find_and_click target for a button whose AX name contains an apostrophe/quote, COPY THE CHARACTER EXACTLY from the AX tree (e.g. 'Let's go' with curly ', not 'Let's go' with straight '). If unsure or if find_and_click returns NONE on first try, fall back to click_at using the button's visible_bbox center. Mac handler returns visible_bbox in points (same coord space click_at expects).
operational_notes.anti_pattern: Do NOT type apostrophes manually when building BT JSON — paste them directly from the AX tree dump or use the AXButton's bbox center via click_at. Common smart-quote characters: ' (U+2018) ' (U+2019) " (U+201C) " (U+201D) — versus ASCII ' (U+0027) and " (U+0022).
operational_notes.example_khan: 'Let’s go' button uses U+2019; find_and_click with ASCII ' returned NONE (verified 2026-05-19).

operational_notes.screen_variant: ANY EXERCISE/QUESTION screen on ANY platform where the question references images, diagrams, charts, or visual states
operational_notes.rule: PHASE: This work belongs in the QUESTION DISCOVERY / EXTRACTION phase, NOT the solve phase. Before any send_to_llm answer call, the BT must have: identified whether images are required, scrolled and captured all relevant frames, and produced per-image descriptions as part of the question context. The solve-phase send_to_llm receives a question that already contains image descriptions and visual reasoning context. IMAGE-DEPENDENT QUESTIONS REQUIRE FULL IMAGE DESCRIPTION IN CONTEXT. On the first look at any question screen, the worker must identify what the question needs to answer correctly. If the question references images, diagrams, before/after states, graphs, charts, or any visual content, those images MUST be described in DETAIL and passed to the LLM as part of the question context — alongside the extracted question text and option list. Image description is AS IMPORTANT AS option extraction. Skipping image analysis or doing it superficially produces wrong answers on visually-grounded questions. Multi-screenshot capture may be required if a single screenshot does not fit the full image — worker must scroll within the question area and take additional screenshots to capture the complete visual context (top + bottom of a tall figure, left + right of a wide diagram, multiple frames of a before/after sequence that exceeds viewport).
operational_notes.scope: UNIVERSAL — every platform, every exercise subtype that uses images.
operational_notes.observed: 2026-05-20: Khan 'Apply: Potential energy' question with TWO image pairs (before/after of a dog being lifted AND before/after of a charged balloon near hair). Question asked about gravitational PE of dog-Earth system AND electric PE of balloon-hair system. LLM picked wrong answers because images weren't analyzed in sufficient detail to determine direction-of-change for each system independently.
operational_notes.bt_template_hint:
CANONICAL BT PHASES for image-dependent questions:
PHASE 1 — DISCOVER (extract phase, before solve):
  1a. extract_question — get question text + option list as today.
  1b. Detect image relevance: AXImage count > 0 in question area OR question text matches "image|figure|diagram|before and after|shown|each system|graph|chart" — if true, proceed to 1c. Otherwise skip to PHASE 2.
  1c. SCROLL + capture sequence: scroll question area top-to-bottom (and left-to-right if a panel is wider than viewport); at each settled position, send_to_llm question_type=solve_complex with prompt "Describe in detail what you see in this view. Note all images, figures, before/after relationships, axis labels, captions." Collect each per-frame description.
  1d. Synthesize: concatenate the per-frame descriptions into a single IMAGE_CONTEXT block.
PHASE 2 — SOLVE:
  2a. send_to_llm question_type=solve_complex with question text + option list + IMAGE_CONTEXT from PHASE 1 + all KB chunks. Instruct: "Use the IMAGE_CONTEXT to ground your answer. For each sub-system referenced in the question, reason independently before producing a final answer."
  2b. Click the chosen option, click Check.
PHASE 3 — VERIFY (post-Check):
  3a. If wrong-answer state observed, route through the wrong-answer-RCA rule (full stop, analyze context gap, do NOT blind-retry).

On any question screen with visible images (AXImage count > 0 in question area, OR question text references 'image' / 'figure' / 'diagram' / 'before and after' / 'shown' / 'each system'), the worker MUST: (1) include the FULL screenshot in send_to_llm (already standard for solve_complex), (2) augment the `question` parameter with explicit instructions: 'Analyze each image in detail. Describe what each image shows BEFORE answering. For before/after image pairs, describe the change between states. For graphs/charts, read axis values and trends.' (3) If multiple distinct sub-systems are referenced in one question (e.g. 'each system' phrasing), instruct the LLM to analyze EACH independently before answering. (4) Use question_type='solve_complex' (vision-enabled) rather than question_type='solve' or 'solve_choice' when images are central to the answer — 'solve_complex' is the canonical vision-grounded path. (5) If the visible viewport does NOT capture the full image (e.g. a tall figure with content below the fold, or a wide diagram clipped on the right), use scroll + need_screenshot to capture additional frames. Pass ALL captured frames to send_to_llm in sequence (multi-image solve_complex). The LLM stitches the views into a complete picture.
operational_notes.anti_pattern: Do NOT use question_type='solve_choice' or 'solve' (text-only) for image-grounded questions. Do NOT pass the question text alone without explicit image-analysis instructions. Do NOT assume the LLM will automatically infer the question is image-dependent — make the instruction explicit in the question parameter. Do NOT rely on a single screenshot when the question references content that may extend below/beside the visible viewport — scroll and re-capture.

operational_notes.screen_variant: ANY wrong-answer outcome on ANY exercise on ANY platform
operational_notes.rule: WRONG ANSWER = CATASTROPHIC FAILURE = FULL ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS REQUIRED. A wrong answer destroys user trust and must never happen without explicit user-set tolerance (e.g. 'I only want 80% right because I'm learning'). When the system detects a wrong-answer state (Try again / Not quite / Incorrect / wrong-answer modal), the response is NOT 'reset and retry' alone — it is FULL STOP and full RCA: what context was sent to the LLM? Was the KB retrieval working and relevant? Were all required images described? Was the question_type appropriate (solve_complex for vision)? Were all options enumerated correctly? Were dropdown discover_menu items captured? THE RESET MECHANISM IS PER-PLATFORM (defined in that platform's knowledge/quirks) — this rule governs the PROCESS (full stop + RCA before any retry), not the mechanics.
operational_notes.scope: UNIVERSAL — applies to every wrong-answer state on every platform.
operational_notes.rationale: Jesse 2026-05-20: 'Opus 4.7 should never get a middle school physics question wrong. They should never get anything wrong on any question on any platform because there is nothing on these, when provided with appropriate context, that you cannot get right every time. Wrong answers cannot happen and all require a full root cause analysis.'
operational_notes.bt_template_hint: WRONG-ANSWER RECOVERY SEQUENCE: (1) Capture FAILURE CONTEXT — read the wrong answer modal's text (if any details), screenshot the current state. (2) STOP — do NOT immediately retry with the same LLM call. (3) ANALYZE: did the LLM have everything it needed? Concretely: (a) Was relevant KB context retrieved (check bt_debug_tail for 'local_kb retrieval: ... top_score ≥ 0.6')? (b) Were images in the question area passed as the screenshot AND with explicit description instructions? (c) Were ALL menu items / options enumerated (check menu_items_seen line)? (d) Did the LLM response cite reasoning consistent with the captured artifacts? (4) IF a context gap is identified, RE-ASK the LLM with the gap filled — e.g. 're-ask with explicit before/after image analysis' or 're-ask with the discovered options pasted verbatim'. (5) Universal Cmd+R reset rule applies for clearing form state before the corrected attempt. (6) ESCALATE if no gap is identifiable — that means the LLM had everything and still got it wrong, which deserves Tier 2 research not a blind retry.
operational_notes.anti_pattern: Do NOT click Try again and immediately re-submit the SAME LLM call expecting different result — the same context produces the same wrong answer. Do NOT treat wrong answers as routine; each one is a system failure deserving analysis. Do NOT skip the RCA step when escalating — the escalation packet must include the LLM's prior answer + the wrong-answer signal so claude-primary can identify the context gap.
